text: right hemicolectomy with D-III lymphadenectomy and intracorporeal anastomosis. The technique involves two operating surgeons, each at their own console, which facilitates rapid dissection and resident teaching. Patient is a sixty-three-year-old gentleman with ascending colon cancer identified on colonoscopy. CT imaging shows no evidence of metastatic disease. The CT scan shows a lesion in the ascending colon.

The surgical team begins the right hemicolectomy using a five-port robotic technique. The omentum is elevated to access the transverse colon and gastrocolic ligament. The gastrocolic ligament is divided along the transverse colon, allowing the omentum to be dissected from the colonic mesentery. Two surgeons collaborate, with one providing traction using graspers while the other dissects the omentum from the transverse colon and mesentery using scissors. The lesser sac is accessed by visualizing the posterior aspect of the stomach, facilitating further dissection of the transverse colon mesentery.

The surgeon begins the D-III lymph node dissection by identifying and dissecting along the superior mesenteric vein, reflecting the D-III lymph nodes to the patient's right for en bloc resection. The ileocolic pedicle is identified, and the right colon mesentery is elevated off the retroperitoneum in a medial-to-lateral fashion, preserving the visceral peritoneum for complete mesocolic excision. The duodenum and head of the pancreas are visualized in relation to the superior mesenteric vein. The ileocolic vein and artery are isolated, ligated, and divided, consistent with central vascular ligation. Dissection continues along the superior mesenteric vein, identifying the trunk of Henle, while preserving the pancreatic branch, and visualizing the middle colic artery.

The surgeon isolates, ligates, and divides the right branch of the middle colic artery. The transverse colon mesentery is divided at the proposed site of transverse colon division, facilitated by prior dissection of the omentum. The attending surgeon retracts with robotic arms while the trainee dissects. The right colon is mobilized by dissecting the final attachments at the hepatic flexure and the right white line of Toldt, medializing the entire right colon and revealing the right ureter and iliac vessels.

The surgeon divides the ileum mesentery to the terminal ileum using a robotic stapler, with the attending surgeon retracting using robotic arms. The terminal ileum is stapled with a 60mm blue load stapler, and the transverse colon is similarly stapled. The specimen is elevated over the liver, and the remaining bowel is prepared for anastomosis. An intracorporeal isoparasotic anastomosis is performed, with the ileum approximated to the transverse colon using robotic arms. A third fire of the stapler creates a common end-running collotomy, which is closed with running Ethabon suture and interrupted Beichel sutures.

The surgeon replaces the omentum and closes the twelve millimeter port site using a suture-pasture technique. The specimen is removed through an upper midline incision, with the option for extraction via a fan and steel incision. The right hemicolectomy is assessed, including the D-III lymphadenectomy.
